findings.title: Segment OF creators into 4 revenue tiers to target the right clients
findings.claim: Agencies should segment potential creator clients into four revenue bands and target the tier where their service delivers the highest ROI.
findings.what_they_do: Dylan segments OF creators into four bands: $0–10K/mo, $10–50K/mo, $100–500K/mo, and $500K+/mo. New/smaller agencies are directed to target the $0–10K band because those creators get the biggest ROI from management services. He sends clients a segmentation sheet and has them identify which band to focus on exclusively, so outreach and pitching is concentrated rather than scattered.
findings.vs_us: Palm has no documented targeting criteria for which creator tier to pursue (§6 GAP). Acquisition is Josh-DM-led with no written segmentation model. This competitor has a concrete framework that tells a new agency owner exactly which creators to approach and why.
findings.recommendation: Build a one-page creator segmentation model for Palm: define the four revenue tiers, articulate the ROI argument for each, and codify which tier(s) Palm currently targets and why. Use this as a qualifier in outbound DM scripts so Josh — and eventually any acquisition hire — is fishing in the right pond from day one.

sources.quote: we split there up into four market segments right only fans models making 500k a month only fans models making between 100 500k a month only fans models making 10 to 50K a month and only fans models anything from 0 to 10K

findings.title: New agencies should master one creator before signing multiple
findings.claim: Beginners who try to sign many clients simultaneously disappoint everyone and burn out; mastering operations on one client first produces better outcomes.
findings.what_they_do: Dylan explicitly advises new agency operators to sign a single creator, test all marketing and chatting strategies on that one account, and only expand once they know what works. He argues that without in-house chatters (which beginners can't afford) and without proven marketing, having five-plus clients is unmanageable and leads to poor results across the board.
findings.vs_us: Palm's acquisition approach is Josh-DM-led without a documented onboarding capacity limit (§6). There is no written rule about how many creators Palm takes on relative to operational readiness (chatting outsourced §8, thin content vault §2). This principle is implicitly understood but not codified as a scaling gate.
findings.recommendation: Define and document Palm's current operational capacity ceiling per creator tier — e.g., how many creators can be served well given current editor headcount, outsourced chat team bandwidth, and social pipeline throughput. Use this as a hard gate in the acquisition process so new signings only happen when capacity exists, preventing the quality dilution Dylan describes.

sources.quote: focus on one client to begin with because a lot of beginners will try and just like scatter gun and try and get as many clients as they can thinking that that is going to solve their problems

findings.title: High-earning creators prioritize comfort over maximum revenue — manage accordingly
findings.claim: Creators earning $40K+/month will willingly sacrifice $5–10K/mo to reduce discomfort with content or workload; agencies that ignore this lose those creators.
findings.what_they_do: Dylan distinguishes two creator mindsets by earnings level. Sub-$40K creators tolerate discomfort to earn more. $40K+ creators who've been in the game a while prioritize comfort — they'll take a $10K/mo hit to avoid content they dislike or a social media workload they resent. His lesson: actively solicit creator feedback, understand the real objection (content type? hours?), and adjust the content/marketing strategy to keep them comfortable even at the cost of some revenue.
findings.vs_us: Palm has no written retention or creator success strategy (§9 GAP). There is no documented framework for tiering creator expectations or proactive comfort-check cadence. This is a practical SOP gap that could cause churn of Palm's highest-value creators.
findings.recommendation: Create a creator retention SOP segmented by earnings tier. For creators above a defined revenue threshold (e.g., $20K+/mo), schedule a monthly 'comfort check' — a structured conversation covering: content they're no longer willing to make, social media hours they find excessive, and any concerns about the agency's direction. Document outcomes in Airtable `Creators` table. Proactively adjust strategy rather than waiting for a creator to signal they're leaving.

sources.quote: keeping your client comfortable with what's being done especially a lot of these higher earning clients that you'll have because they're making so much money money's no longer the focus for them it's making money while being comfortable at the same time

findings.title: Identify and solve one operational bottleneck at a time rather than fixing everything at once
findings.claim: Agencies scale most effectively by diagnosing the single largest bottleneck — social coverage, chatter quality, chatter hours — and solving only that before moving to the next.
findings.what_they_do: Dylan walks through a sequential bottleneck-resolution framework: (1) Could more social accounts drive more revenue? Yes → outsource social. (2) Could 24/7 chatting earn more? Yes → hire chatters. (3) Could better-trained chatters earn more? Yes → build chatter training. Each step is solved fully before the next is attempted. He explicitly warns against trying to solve all problems simultaneously, which leads to overwhelm and paralysis.
findings.vs_us: Palm is actively running multiple parallel initiatives (SMM hub redesign §11, AI chatting project §8, warmup improvements §4, Phase 3 Publer §5). The risk Dylan describes — solving everything at once — is live. Palm does not have a documented prioritization framework for which bottleneck to tackle first.
findings.recommendation: Adopt a formal bottleneck log in Airtable or the admin portal: a ranked list of operational constraints with a single 'active focus' flag. At each leadership sync, confirm only one bottleneck is in active resolution. This isn't about slowing down — it's about ensuring each fix is fully deployed and measured before the next one starts. Apply this now to sequence the SMM consolidation, chat buildout, and Phase 3 Publer work.

sources.quote: look at where your bottlenecks are in your business and work from there so you know could you make more money with a client if you had more social media accounts running traffic yes okay well how do you do that

findings.title: Twitter/X is currently the strongest traffic platform; Reddit is high-potential but underutilized
findings.claim: Among organic traffic platforms, Twitter/X currently delivers the best measurable results for OF creator traffic, with Reddit expected to outperform once properly activated.
findings.what_they_do: Dylan's agency client reports running Twitter (short teaser clips, slightly explicit), Reddit (entering subreddits, not yet fully active), Instagram (teaser photos + Reels), and TikTok-to-recreate. Twitter is confirmed as currently performing best. Dylan validates that Reddit, once properly worked, will be the strongest traffic source. He explicitly calls out that copy-paste trending TikTok tactics from Telegram groups are 'super outdated' — TikTok is no longer the viral free-for-all it was in 2022.
findings.vs_us: Palm has no documented traffic strategy across platforms (§5 GAP). The tech spine routes content via Publer and Telegram but there's no prioritization of which platforms drive OF conversions or how to activate Reddit. This finding gives a concrete platform hierarchy: Twitter first (activate and optimize), Reddit second (build out properly), IG ongoing.
findings.recommendation: Document Palm's traffic platform hierarchy explicitly. Prioritize: (1) Ensure every real creator has an active Twitter/X account with teaser clips being posted — confirm this is happening and measure click-through to OF. (2) Build a Reddit activation SOP: subreddit research, posting cadence, account warming to avoid bans (§platform-risk overlap). (3) Formally retire the copy-paste trending TikTok Telegram-group method as a primary strategy; replace with the data-driven inspo pipeline Palm already has (§1).

sources.quote: Twitter's the best yeah Twitter okay so Twitter's the best okay um Reddit though would weigh when we start getting that going so I know that's very good for traffic
that's like I would say that's like in my opinion like a super outdated method like it's very like TikTok is completely different people don't understand

findings.title: Proactively explain 'why' behind every agency decision to prevent creator churn from miscommunication
findings.claim: Most creator-agency relationship problems stem from unclear communication about what the agency does and why, not from the tactics themselves.
findings.what_they_do: Dylan's top lesson is that agencies fail creators by not explaining the reasoning behind decisions. When a creator complains or pushes back, the wrong response is 'we just do this because it works.' The right response is to genuinely understand what's bothering them — is it the content type? The social media time demand? The lack of transparency? He builds in active listening and clear explanation as a core operational habit, not an ad-hoc fix.
findings.vs_us: Palm's creator onboarding captures basics (form wizard, contract, voice memo — §6) but there is no documented ongoing communication cadence or SOP for handling creator objections/concerns. As Palm scales, this gap will compound — creators managed at arm's length without regular 'why' explanations will churn faster.
findings.recommendation: Add two communication elements to Palm's creator management SOP: (1) An onboarding 'strategy brief' call where Palm explains the content pipeline, posting cadence, and chatting approach — and why each element works — before anything goes live. (2) A recurring creator check-in (bi-weekly or monthly) with a structured agenda: results review, upcoming content plan, anything the creator is uncomfortable with. Template these in Airtable against the `Creators` table.

sources.quote: a lot of issues that are going to arise are going to be from you not communicating clearly with your client on like what you do and just setting expectations framing stuff the correct way

findings.title: Master one marketing channel completely before adding others
findings.claim: Agencies that spread effort across many marketing channels become mediocre at all of them; depth in one channel outperforms breadth across many.
findings.what_they_do: Dylan's second top lesson: pick one traffic/marketing method and become the best at it before expanding. He acknowledges the risk of single-channel dependency (e.g., TikTok declining) but frames transferable skills as the safety net — skills learned on TikTok apply to Instagram Reels or YouTube Shorts. The discipline is still to go deep on one channel first, then adapt skills to adjacent channels when the primary shows diminishing returns.
findings.vs_us: Palm's social pipeline (§1) is built around Instagram/Reels as the primary channel with some TikTok. The inspo-scraping system is IG-centric. This is aligned with Dylan's advice. However, Palm has no documented decision framework for when to add a new channel or deprecate an underperforming one. The gap is strategic channel governance, not channel selection itself.
findings.recommendation: Document Palm's primary channel as Instagram Reels (current de facto state) and define explicit criteria for adding a second channel — e.g., 'when the IG pipeline is running at full cadence for 90 days with consistent growth, evaluate Reddit or TikTok as channel two.' This prevents premature channel sprawl and gives the team clear focus. Also document skill-transfer logic: the IG Reels inspo pipeline (§1) is reusable for TikTok and YouTube Shorts — make this explicit so editors understand the transferability.

sources.quote: lesson two is always going to go back to marketing so many people are trying to do everything yeah just focus on one thing and become the best at it

findings.topic: Meta ads as primary traffic channel
findings.title: Run scripted Meta video ads direct-to-OF to bypass IG algorithm dependency
findings.claim: Purpose-built Meta ad creatives linked directly to OnlyFans via the OF tracking API can deliver 50–100 paid subs per day per creator without relying on organic IG reach.
findings.what_they_do: Agency writes 5–10 bespoke video ad scripts per model (e.g. 'boyfriend films her scrolling OF messages' scenario). Creator films them. Agency runs a $40/day testing phase across all creatives for 7 days, then identifies best cost-per-acquisition winner. Winning creative gets scaled with increased budget. Links go directly to OF using the OnlyFans API tracking link, with Meta pixel attached so both subscriber volume and spend are measurable. No intermediate redirect links needed.
findings.vs_us: Palm has no documented paid-traffic system (§5 🔲 GAP — traffic/distribution strategy not documented). Palm's current funnel is organic social → OF. This is a fully orthogonal paid channel that de-risks the IG dependency Palm currently has.
findings.recommendation: Build a Meta ads playbook: (1) create a script bank of 5–10 ad concepts per creator, (2) run a structured 7-day/$40-per-day creative test, (3) integrate OF API tracking links + Meta pixel for CPA visibility, (4) scale winning creative. Start with one creator as a pilot. This directly addresses the IG instability risk flagged in §1 and §5.

sources.quote: we will give creators ad scripts to make
we'll do $40 per day every single day, and we'll see which one the algorithm actually favors
we literally just use OnlyFans API. We get a tracking link from there, so we can track everything, we can add the pixel in there

findings.topic: Cloud phone farm for IG account amplification
findings.title: Operate 200–300 cloud-based IG accounts to funnel traffic to creator main pages
findings.claim: Running hundreds of warmed-up Instagram accounts via cloud phones on automated schedules generates 50–200 additional subs per day per creator while insulating the main account from bans.
findings.what_they_do: Agency spins up 200–300 Instagram accounts on cloud-hosted real phones. Automations handle: feed scrolling, liking, Reel posting, phone-number verification — all to mimic organic human behavior and avoid suspensions. None of these satellite accounts carry the OF link in bio; they funnel only to the creator's main IG page (or to 5 intermediate accounts). The OF link lives only on the main page. Suspension rate drops to ~1-in-20 vs. higher rates when the OF link is in satellite bios. If main account is banned, warmed satellite accounts are handed to the creator to restart on.
findings.vs_us: Palm has a mature AI-account warmup system for AI personas (§3/§4) using GrapheneOS Pixels and Publer, but this is for synthetic creator accounts, not a satellite-funnel network for real creators. Palm has no documented equivalent for real-creator traffic amplification (§4 🔲 GAP — real-creator warmup/cadence not formalized; §5 🔲 GAP — traffic strategy not documented).
findings.recommendation: Evaluate adopting a scaled cloud-phone funnel for real creators. Palm's existing hardware-isolated warmup infrastructure (GrapheneOS, SIM inventory, Publer) gives a meaningful head start — the playbook could be adapted. Key decisions: (1) cloud phone provider selection, (2) automation tooling for feed warm-up, (3) funnel topology (satellite → main IG → OF). Start small (20–30 accounts for one creator) before scaling to 200+. Flag as high-priority given current IG instability signals.

sources.quote: we set up hundreds of Instagram accounts... you can run everything off the cloud
In none of these accounts' bios, we're putting that link... we're just funneling to other Instagram pages
if we have 200 phones for one creator, and we're literally pulling one subscriber per day from each of the phones, then that's 200 subs a day

findings.topic: IG ban survivability via satellite account bench
findings.title: Pre-warm satellite IG accounts as instant replacement bench when main account is banned
findings.claim: Maintaining a pool of continuously warmed satellite accounts means a creator can immediately resume posting on a new account the day their main is banned, with zero cold-start penalty.
findings.what_they_do: Because satellite accounts are already being warmed daily (feed scrolling, posting, engagement), they have account age and trust score built up. When the creator's primary IG account is banned, the agency hands over one or more of these pre-warmed accounts for the creator to continue posting from immediately, avoiding the traffic gap that causes creator churn.
findings.vs_us: Palm's AI-persona warmup system (§3/§4) achieves exactly this for synthetic creators — a 90-day warmup produces a ready-to-use account. The same logic is not applied to real-creator backup accounts (§4 🔲 GAP). This is a direct extension of Palm's existing capability to the real-creator line.
findings.recommendation: Maintain a small bench (3–5) of pre-warmed IG accounts per real creator. Palm's existing warmup operator UI and Publer scheduling could drive this with minimal incremental tooling. Define a handoff SOP: when main account ban is confirmed, assign bench account to creator within 24 hours. This directly reduces model churn from traffic loss (a live pain point per §6 — model retention).

sources.quote: if the creators' main accounts do get banned, we have a lot of other accounts which we can just give them, and they can start posting on them, because we've been growing these accounts anyway

findings.title: Keep OF link off satellite accounts — funnel to intermediate IG pages only
findings.claim: Placing the OF link only on the main creator page (not on satellite/funnel accounts) reduces satellite account suspension rate to approximately 1-in-20.
findings.what_they_do: Satellite accounts never carry any OF or external link in their bios. Their sole job is to drive IG profile visits to the creator's main account (or a set of 5 intermediate accounts). The OF link lives exclusively at the final destination page. This separation keeps satellite accounts off Meta's adult-content radar and dramatically lowers ban rates.
findings.vs_us: Palm's current documented setup (§5) routes traffic through a single creator IG → link-in-bio → OF. The satellite layer with link-segregation is not present. Palm's AI persona accounts do use Beacons link-in-bio (§3), which may face similar flagging risk if OF is referenced. This architecture principle — isolate the OF link to as few accounts as possible — is directly applicable.
findings.recommendation: Audit current Palm creator IG setups: does every account that references OF carry the link? If so, consider a two-tier architecture where traffic-generation accounts funnel to a single 'conversion' account that holds the OF link. This reduces surface area for platform enforcement actions across the entire portfolio.

sources.quote: in none of these accounts' bios, we're putting that link... we're just funneling to other Instagram pages, so our accounts can stay way safer
The suspension rate is probably one in 20 accounts, but it's way lower than if you actually have the link in the bio

findings.topic: Traffic continuity as model retention lever
findings.title: Treat consistent traffic delivery as the #1 model retention mechanism
findings.claim: Agencies that maintain traffic through IG downturns retain their creators; agencies that can't keep traffic flowing lose models and revenue simultaneously.
findings.what_they_do: This agency explicitly frames multi-channel traffic infrastructure (Meta ads + phone farm) not just as a growth tool but as a creator relationship tool. When IG accounts get restricted, creators see revenue drop and blame the agency. By having non-IG traffic running continuously, the agency absorbs IG volatility without it becoming a creator-facing crisis. The result: agency kept all models during a period when competitors lost creators, and received referrals from satisfied models.
findings.vs_us: Palm has no documented creator retention strategy (§9 🔲 GAP) and no multi-channel traffic system (§5 🔲 GAP). If Palm's real creators are currently IG-dependent for traffic, they face the same churn risk described here. The connection between traffic reliability and model retention is not captured in Palm's current framework.
findings.recommendation: Add 'traffic continuity during IG downtime' as an explicit SLA in creator agreements and as a tracked KPI in the whale-hunting / fan-tracker dashboards (§9). Build the narrative internally: Palm's job is to deliver traffic regardless of platform conditions. Use this framing in creator acquisition pitches (§6) as a differentiator vs. single-channel agencies.

sources.quote: Models are leaving, your revenue is going down... we've been able to keep all of our models, even get referrals, whilst most agencies are losing their creators
you should be providing them traffic even when things are bad

findings.title: Commission creator-filmed ad creatives from agency-written scripts, not boosted organic posts
findings.claim: Boosting existing organic posts performs poorly as paid traffic; scripted video ads designed specifically for conversion (with hard CTAs) significantly outperform.
findings.what_they_do: Agency writes 5–10 distinct video ad scripts per model designed for paid promotion — not repurposed from organic content. Scripts follow a narrative/scenario structure (e.g. boyfriend-behind-camera POV, creator implied to be on OF). Each includes a hard CTA so viewers understand she has an OF page. Creator films all scripts as a batch. Agency tests all creatives at $40/day for 7 days, identifies winner(s), requests variation batch of the winner format, and continues iterating. Distinction from organic content pipeline: purpose, format, and CTA are all different.
findings.vs_us: Palm has a strong data-driven organic content pipeline (§1) but no equivalent system for paid ad creatives — these are a distinct content type (neither 'social top-of-funnel' nor 'OF vault/PPV' in Palm's current taxonomy; they're a third type: paid acquisition creatives). §2 notes Palm is thin on OF vault content; this is a fourth content category Palm currently has no system for.
findings.recommendation: Define 'paid ad creative' as a formal content type in Palm's content taxonomy alongside organic social and OF vault. Build a script bank template (scenario types, CTA formulas). Add an 'ad creative' task type in the Airtable content ops table (`applLIT2t83plMqNx`) so creators can be assigned scripted ad filming tasks distinct from organic reel tasks. This is the content-production prerequisite for the Meta ads traffic channel.

sources.quote: I'm talking about actual creatives made specifically for this
we'll give five to 10 scripts for video ads per model... we'll run a testing phase for these ad creatives

findings.title: Score every chatter on 5 KPIs to build a leaderboard before restructuring
findings.claim: Pulling 30 days of chat logs and scoring each chatter on response time, PPV buy rate, average fan spend, retention, and objection handling creates an objective leaderboard that drives all restructuring decisions.
findings.what_they_do: Pull last 30 days of chat logs for every chatter. Score each individual across: response time, PPV buy rate, average fan spend, retention rate, objection handling quality. Aggregate into a single leaderboard score per chatter. Use that ranked list as the sole basis for warnings, fires, and promotions.
findings.vs_us: Palm currently outsources all chatting to an external team (§8) and has no internal chatter KPI framework or leaderboard. This audit methodology is exactly the kind of build-toward intelligence needed when Palm moves to an in-house chat team.
findings.recommendation: When building the in-house chat team, implement this 5-KPI audit from day one. Define the scoring rubric before hiring chatter #1 so the leaderboard is operational at scale. Consider requiring the external chat team to report these same KPIs now as a baseline.

sources.quote: pull the last 30 days of chat logs for every single chatter and score them individually
you now have a list of all your chatters with an overall score. You now have a leaderboard

findings.title: 30-day written warning for bottom-20% chatters; immediate fire for worst performers
findings.claim: After scoring chatters, bottom 20% should receive either an immediate termination (truly poor) or a formal 30-day written warning with a clear performance improvement expectation.
findings.what_they_do: After building the leaderboard: worst performers are fired immediately. Bottom 20% who show potential receive a written warning stating explicitly that they have 30 days to improve or they will be replaced. No ambiguity — the timeline and consequence are stated upfront.
findings.vs_us: Palm has no internal chatter management process (§8, outsourced). For the in-house roadmap, this is a concrete PIP (Performance Improvement Plan) template to adopt.
findings.recommendation: Draft a written warning template for chatter PIPs before scaling the in-house team. Include the 5 KPI scores, the target thresholds, the 30-day deadline, and the consequence. Pair it with a termination checklist so the process is consistent regardless of who manages it.

sources.quote: take the 20% who are either going to get a written warning or you're just going to let them go
You have 30 days. Get your shit together

findings.title: Promote top-20% chatters to shift leads who own whale accounts and implement systems first
findings.claim: Top-performing chatters should be elevated to shift leads with higher-value responsibilities — handling whales, receiving best subscribers, implementing new systems, and potentially earning better pay — to incentivize excellence without necessarily moving them to pure management.
findings.what_they_do: Top 20% by leaderboard score become shift leads. Responsibilities: handle all whale interactions, assigned the highest-value subscribers, first cohort to test and implement any new system or script, eligible for increased pay tied to sustained performance. They remain chatters but are the internal standard-setters.
findings.vs_us: No internal chatter structure exists at Palm (§8). This shift-lead model is a concrete org design to adopt when building the in-house team — it creates a promotion path without pulling best chatters away from revenue-generating work.
findings.recommendation: Design the in-house chat org with two tiers from the start: chatters and shift leads. Write the shift-lead job description now (whale ownership, system rollout, quality oversight) so the role is clear before the first hire. Tie shift-lead pay to whale revenue metrics, not just tenure.

sources.quote: the top 20%, so the best of the best, they become shift leads. They have the responsibility to handle whales

findings.title: Standardize all scripts and objection-handling so mid-conversation shift handoffs are seamless
findings.claim: Without fully standardized scripts across shifts, handoffs mid-selling-sequence cause subscribers to disengage, and the agency loses $1–2K per day specifically at shift-change moments.
findings.what_they_do: Every script, objection-handling sequence, and selling approach is written down and standardized across all shifts. The standard is specific enough that if Chatter A stops mid-sentence, Chatter B can pick up the exact conversation thread without the subscriber noticing a change. Revenue tracking consistently shows the lowest revenue window is at shift change — this is the explicit problem they are solving.
findings.vs_us: Palm outsources chatting (§8) and has no internal script or handoff SOP. This is a critical build-toward finding: the revenue leak at shift change is quantified ($1–2K/day at scale) and the fix is documentation, not hiring.
findings.recommendation: When designing the in-house chat system, treat shift-handoff continuity as a first-class requirement. Build a conversation-state handoff field (current PPV sequence step, last offer made, fan emotional state, open objections) into whatever CRM or chat tool is used. Standardize scripts before hiring chatters — not after.

sources.quote: standardize the scripts, standardize the objection handling, the selling ways. So if one chatter stops texting mid-sentence, the other chatter can jump in flawlessly
it's always the lowest revenue right when the shift shifts

findings.title: Cross-train all chatters on a single universal persona tone, especially for whale handoffs
findings.claim: Whales who experience a persona or tone shift at handoff disengage and churn — every chatter must be trained to voice the same persona regardless of which shift they are on.
findings.what_they_do: All chatters are cross-trained on one universal tone and persona voice. Whale interactions are treated as the highest-risk handoff: if Chatter A builds rapport with a whale and Chatter B takes over sounding different, the whale is lost. They explicitly state they have lost 'a few good whales' from this failure. The fix is a documented tone/persona guide that is part of chatter onboarding and ongoing training.
findings.vs_us: Palm has a whale-hunting and Fan Tracker CRM system (§9) but no internal chatter voice/persona standard (§8, outsourced). For the AI-persona line (§3), Palm already thinks in terms of a consistent synthetic persona — that same discipline needs to carry into the chat layer when brought in-house.
findings.recommendation: Create a persona voice guide for each creator before the in-house chat team goes live. Define: vocabulary/slang the creator uses, emotional register, how she responds to high spenders vs. new subs, hard boundaries on what she'd 'say.' Make this guide mandatory reading at chatter onboarding. For AI personas (§3), this guide already partially exists — extract and formalize it.

sources.quote: they all need to be cross-trained and they need to talk in the same persona. There's one universal tone on how to talk to people, especially whales
